---
title: "Methods"
subtitle: "From the most basic to the most complex — the metric, the CV spine, the features, the GBDT, the image experts, and the stack"
toc: true
---

This page is deliberately verbose and bottom-up. We start with the single most important and
most misunderstood thing — the metric — and build up to the full hybrid pipeline.

## The metric: partial AUC above 80% TPR {#the-metric}

### Why not accuracy, and why not plain AUC?

At 0.098% prevalence, **accuracy is meaningless** (predict "benign" always → 99.9% accurate,
zero malignant caught). Plain **ROC-AUC** is better — it is threshold-free and prevalence-robust
— but it averages performance over *all* operating points, including useless low-sensitivity
ones. A clinical triage tool only matters where it **catches almost every cancer**: the
high-sensitivity tail.

### The definition

The official ISIC-2024 metric is the **partial area under the ROC curve, restricted to the
region where the true-positive rate (sensitivity) is at least 80%**:

$$
\text{pAUC}_{80} \;=\; \int_{\text{TPR}=0.80}^{1.0} \text{TPR}\; d(\text{FPR})
$$

Equivalently: integrate the ROC curve only over the band TPR ∈ [0.80, 1.0] (FPR ≤ 0.20), then
report the area in **TPR-units × FPR-units**. Because the band is 0.20 tall, the score is bounded
in **`[0, 0.20]`**:

- **random** classifier ≈ **0.02**,
- **perfect** classifier = **0.20**.

A move from 0.169 → 0.174 is therefore a **+2.9% relative** gain on a small absolute scale —
small absolute moves are large relative moves, and we phrase improvements that way throughout.

### Two pitfalls we pinned down up front

::: {.callout-warning}
## The 80% vs 88% trap
The public ISIC metrics repository's README mentions a **88% TPR** threshold (which would bound
the score in `[0, 0.12]`), but the **live competition used 80%**. Mixing the two makes any
number incomparable. We **pin 80%** for every leaderboard-comparable claim, and our scorer is
hard-coded to it.
:::

We do not re-implement the metric loosely. `src/cv.py:pauc_above_tpr` is the **only** function
allowed to compute it, and it is proven **numerically identical** to the vendored official
scorer (`src/metric_official.py`, © 2024 N. R. Kurtansky, MSKCC) by a unit test
(`tests/test_cv.py`, part of the 9/9 passing suite).

## The CV spine: the whole game {#cv-spine}

In ISIC-2024, **leaky cross-validation sent teams ~200 places down** on the private split.
Validation is not a step here — it is the spine everything hangs on.

- **Patient-grouped, target-stratified folds.** We use `StratifiedGroupKFold` (5 folds, SEED =
  42) with the **patient** as the group and the **malignant label** as the stratification target.
  - *Grouped* → **no patient straddles folds** (a patient's lesions are correlated; a single
    straddling patient leaks).
  - *Stratified* → each fold holds 77–83 of the 393 positives, so no fold is starved of signal.
- **Frozen once.** The split is written to `data/folds.parquet` and read, **unchanged**, by
  every expert and by the stack. There is exactly one split in the entire project.
- **Hard no-leak guarantee + veto.** The `cv-guardian` agent has **veto power** over anything
  touching the split or the metric: if a change would leak patients across folds, recompute the
  metric independently, or compare models on mismatched splits, it must block. This is the
  anti-shakeup discipline that makes our OOF number trustworthy.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Out-of-fold (OOF) predictions.** For each fold we train on the other four and predict on the
held-out fold; concatenating the five held-out predictions gives one prediction per row, none of
which ever saw its own row in training. We score pAUC **once** on this full OOF vector. Because
pAUC is non-additive across folds, the full-OOF number (0.17376) differs trivially from the mean
of per-fold pAUCs (0.17487) — both are reported.
:::

## Tabular feature engineering {#tabular-fe}

The predictive signal lives largely in the **tabular metadata**. `src/features.py` builds, from
ISIC-2024 columns only (no external data, every feature leak-checked):

- **Geometry & size ratios** — normalized lesion size, perimeter, area, eccentricity, axis
  ratios, border composites; raw size is strong, ratios make it scale-robust.
- **Color / hue / luminance contrasts** — `tbp_lv_H` (hue angle), lesion-vs-skin contrast,
  color uniformity, ΔA/ΔB color deltas. Hue alone reaches univariate AUC 0.81.
- **Border & shape composites** — irregularity and asymmetry combinations.
- **3D position** — the lesion's location on the body surface (TBP gives true 3D coordinates).
- **Patient-relative "ugly-duckling" deviations — the decisive block.** For each lesion and each
  base feature, how anomalous is it *relative to the same patient's other lesions*?
  - `pdev_*` — deviation (signed/standardized distance from the patient's own mean),
  - `prank_*` — within-patient percentile rank,
  - `pxc_*` — patient × category interactions (e.g. hue × body location).
  - These encode the clinical "ugly-duckling sign" directly. They turn out to be **~65% of total
    GBDT gain** (see [Results](results.qmd) and [Ablations](ablations.qmd)).
- **Per-fold smoothed target encoding** — for high-cardinality categoricals (e.g. location),
  computed **inside each fold's training data only** and applied to the held-out fold, with
  smoothing toward the global mean.

::: {.callout-important}
## Leak-safety of features
Every patient-relative statistic and every target encoding is computed **fold-locally** (from
training rows only) and applied to held-out rows — so no held-out information ever flows into a
feature. The `cv-guardian` audits this. Patient-relative deviations are safe because they use
the *patient's own benign moles*, which are in the same fold by construction (patients don't
straddle folds).
:::

## The GBDT expert {#gbdt}

`src/gbdt.py` trains the tabular expert. Two stories matter here — one cautionary, one
constructive.

### The underfit bug (and the fix)

Our first GBDT scored **0.09941** — barely 5× random. The cause was a classic imbalance
foot-gun: `is_unbalance=True` **up-weighted the 393 positives ~1000×**, and the LightGBM
early-stopping was driven by **built-in AUC**, which under that weighting peaked at
**`best_iter = 1`** — the model essentially never trained.

The fix, in two parts:

1. **Drop `is_unbalance`** — let undersampling (below) handle the imbalance instead of extreme
   instance weights.
2. **Early-stop on the official pAUC** — a custom `feval` in `src/gbdt.py` evaluates pAUC@80%TPR
   directly, so early stopping optimizes the metric we are actually scored on.

This single change took the model from 0.09941 → 0.11826 (+0.019). See
[Ablations → Tabular](ablations.qmd#tabular).

### The production recipe

The final tabular expert (greysky-lineage hyperparameters; see [Credits](credits.qmd)) is a
**bagged ensemble**:

- **Manual undersampling** to ~1% (`neg_ratio = 0.01` → ~100 negatives per positive, ~4k rows
  per fold/seed) so each booster trains fast and is not drowned by benigns.
- **5-seed bagging** (seeds 12/22/32/42/52) of LightGBM, averaged on rank.
- **CatBoost rank-blend** — a second GBDT family bagged the same way; the final tabular OOF is
  `0.8·rank(LightGBM-bag) + 0.2·rank(CatBoost-bag)`. CatBoost is the weaker family here, so an
  equal 0.5 blend dilutes (0.16809); 0.2 is the swept optimum (**0.16890**).

Result: **OOF pAUC 0.16890** at essentially zero inference cost (0.86 M "params" of trees,
~0 GFLOPs, 0.02 ms/img). This is the **efficient anchor** of the whole frontier.

## The image experts {#image-experts}

`src/vision/*` trains the deep protagonist. Each backbone is one point on the efficiency
frontier, measured by the `efficiency-auditor`.

### The recipe (shared across backbones)

- **Small, natural-image-pretrained backbones** at **128 px** (and 224 px for the resolution
  axis). Pretraining is ImageNet / FCMAE only — **no skin-cancer labels**, so the
  no-external-*data* claim survives (pretrained *weights* are allowed; external *training data*
  is not).
- **Per-epoch negative undersampling to ~1:1** (`neg_ratio`, `pos_mult`). Each epoch sees all
  positives + a fresh random ~equal sample of negatives, so an "epoch" is only a few thousand
  images and runs in **~4.5 s**. A full 5-fold backbone trains in **~8 minutes**. Re-sampling
  the negatives each epoch means the model still sees the whole benign distribution over time.
- **Loss:** BCE with **label smoothing 0.05** (softens the 393 hard positives).
- **Augmentation:** the `transV2` policy (flips, rotations, color jitter within reason —
  classical only, **no generative augmentation**).
- **EMA(0.995) + mixup(α=0.2).** Weight EMA stabilizes the noisy undersampled updates; mixup
  regularizes. **EMA decay matters:** at ~23 steps/epoch, decay 0.999 (time-constant ~1000
  steps) *lags* the weights and **hurts** (fold-0: 0.146 vs 0.156); **0.995 + warmup helps**
  (0.163). Pinned at 0.995.
- **Resolution as a frontier axis.** We keep both `convnextv2_nano@128` (0.15311) and `@224`
  (0.15821) as distinct points, because resolution trades accuracy for cost.

### The 12-backbone frontier sweep

We swept a family spanning the cost axis from ~2.5 M to ~294 M params:
`mnv4_small`, `effvit_b0`, `starnet_s1`, `fastvit_t8`, `effnetv2_b0`, `ghostnetv3`,
`vit_tiny`, `vit_small`, `convnextv2_nano` (128 px and 224 px), `convnextv2_tiny`,
`swinv2_tiny`, `eva02_small`, and the heavy anchor `mnv5_300m`. The clear lesson (see
[Results](results.qmd) and [Ablations](ablations.qmd)): **heavy transformers collapse to
near-random** at 393 positives (SwinV2 &#64;256 → 0.104, EVA-02 &#64;336 → 0.100), while the **small**
`convnextv2_nano` is the sweet spot.

### Self-supervised pretraining — the principled "one-up" (future work)

The cleanest in-constraint way to exploit the 400k+ **unlabeled** crops — without importing
external data or fabricating positives — is **self-supervised pretraining** (MAE / DINOv2-style)
on the crops themselves, then fine-tuning on the 393 labels. This substitutes *our own
unlabeled data* for the external data we banned, and stays fully inside the rules. We present
it as the principled next step rather than a completed result; it is the most promising lever we
did not exhaust in the 2-day budget.

## The stack (combiner) {#stack}

`src/stack.py` fuses the experts. We compared three combiners on the same OOF vectors:

| Combiner | What it does | OOF pAUC |
|---|---|---:|
| **Rank-average** | average the percentile ranks of GBDT and image OOF — **param-free** | **0.17376** |
| Meta-LightGBM | a second-level GBDT over the expert OOFs | 0.17108 |
| Learned per-lesion gate (MoE) | a network picks expert weights per lesion | 0.15007 |

We also tried adding the **image embedding** (PCA components) and an **image-space ugly-duckling**
feature into the GBDT; both **hurt** (< 0.1738).

::: {.callout-note}
## Why the trivial combiner wins at 393 positives
A rank-average has **zero parameters to fit**, so it cannot overfit the validation folds. Every
learned combiner has to estimate its parameters from the *same 393 positives* the experts
already used — there is simply not enough positive signal left to learn a better fusion than
"average the ranks." The learned gate even falls **below the tabular expert alone** (0.150 <
0.169). This is the project's recurring theme, and it is a *result*, not a disappointment:
**at this scale, simpler is both cheaper and more accurate.** Full numbers in
[Ablations](ablations.qmd).
:::

## Efficiency as a first-class axis

Every reported model logs **params, FLOPs, and single-thread CPU latency** (`src/efficiency.py`)
alongside its pAUC, so each model is one point on a **quality-vs-cost plane**. The Pareto-optimal
set — points that no other model beats on *both* axes — is the headline figure. A model that
does not earn its cost does not stay in the pipeline. See [Results](results.qmd).

---

*Continue to [Results →](results.qmd)*
